text.title: Solution Assessment Failure Modes
text.summary: Common failure modes in solution assessment: missing baselines, requirements drift, variance misread as causation, adoption confused with value, and single-snapshot overconfidence.

antipattern.description: Capturing the baseline at assessment time rather than at requirements freeze.
antipattern.reason: The "baseline" silently becomes "yesterday" and variance is zero by construction — the assessment proves nothing and is unfalsifiable.

antipattern.description: Treating requirements compliance as equivalent to value realization: reporting "100% requirements met" without measuring business outcomes.
antipattern.reason: A solution can be 100% requirements-met and 0% value-realized; the report must surface the functional-met vs benefit-realized split explicitly.

antipattern.description: Reporting metric variance (e.g., revenue +18%) as caused by the solution without controlling for external factors.
antipattern.reason: Market tailwinds, competitor outages, or compensation-plan changes can produce the same variance. Without a control group or diff-in-diff, confidence must be marked low and external factors flagged.

antipattern.description: Running a single 30-day post-launch assessment and treating it as definitive.
antipattern.reason: At 30 days, users are in honeymoon mode; 12-month assessments consistently show lower satisfaction and surfaced friction that the 30-day report misses. One-shot assessments systematically overstate success.

antipattern.description: Assessing adoption (usage rate) as a proxy for value delivered.
antipattern.reason: Mandatory rollouts always show high adoption; only paired cycle-time or output metrics reveal whether the adoption was beneficial or destructive.

antipattern.description: User satisfaction surveys that do not reconcile survey respondent count against active-user count.
antipattern.reason: Surveys reach still-active users; n=50 with a 4.2 score can hide 200 silent leavers. Always cross-reference survey n against active user count; reject satisfaction claims where n/active-users is below a documented threshold (e.g., 10%).

rule.statement: Force every requirements compliance row to include an evidence_ref that resolves to a source; drop any row where evidence is missing rather than inventing a value.
rule.rationale: LLMs will fill in "actual" values when evidence is missing; fabricated metrics produce false accept/reject verdicts.

rule.statement: Generate the recommendation as a single enum (accept | accept_with_conditions | require_changes | reject) before any prose; prose must be derived from, not instead of, the enum.
rule.rationale: LLM recommendation prose hedges by default ("consider exploring"); forcing an enum first prevents verdict ambiguity.
